title: Citizenship · Congress & the President
description: The legislative and executive branches: Congress, Senators, Representatives, the President, the Cabinet, and how a bill becomes law.

cards.front: Name one branch or part of the government.
cards.back: Congress (legislative), the President (executive), or the courts (judicial). The Constitution divides federal power among these three branches.
cards.quizAnswer: Congress, the President, or the courts.
distractors: The Cabinet, the states, and the military.
The Senate, the House, and the Supreme Court.
The federal, state, and local governments.
The police, the army, and the courts.
The President, the Vice President, and Congress.

cards.front: What stops one branch of government from becoming too powerful?
cards.back: Checks and balances, also called the separation of powers. Each branch can limit the actions of the others, so no single branch dominates.
distractors: The right of the people to vote.
The power of the Supreme Court alone.
The President's veto over all laws.
The federal system of fifty states.

cards.front: Who is in charge of the executive branch?
cards.back: The President. The executive branch enforces and carries out the laws that Congress makes.

cards.front: Who makes federal laws?
cards.back: Congress, made up of the Senate and the House of Representatives. The President signs bills into law, but Congress writes and passes them.
cards.quizAnswer: Congress (the Senate and the House of Representatives).
distractors: The President alone by executive order.

cards.front: What are the two parts of the U.S. Congress?
cards.back: The Senate and the House of Representatives. This two-house structure is called a bicameral legislature.
cards.quizAnswer: The Senate and the House of Representatives.
distractors: The Senate and the Supreme Court.
The upper court and the lower court.
The federal and the state houses.
The Congress and the Electoral College.

cards.front: How many U.S. Senators are there?
cards.back: One hundred (100). Each state elects two Senators, regardless of the state's population.

cards.front: We elect a U.S. Senator for how many years?
cards.back: Six (6) years. Senate terms are staggered, so about one-third of the Senate is up for election every two years.

cards.front: Who is one of your state's U.S. Senators now?
cards.back: Answers vary by state. Look up your state's two U.S. Senators before your interview — you must name one of them, not your governor or representative. (Residents of D.C. and territories may answer that they have no U.S. Senators.)
cards.quizAnswer: One of the two U.S. Senators elected from your state.
distractors: The governor who leads your state.
Your member of the U.S. House.
One of your two state senators.
The mayor of your largest city.
Either of your state's two electors.

cards.front: The House of Representatives has how many voting members?
cards.back: Four hundred thirty-five (435). The number of Representatives from each state is based on the state's population.

cards.front: We elect a U.S. Representative for how many years?
cards.back: Two (2) years. The entire House is up for election every two years.

cards.front: Name your U.S. Representative.
cards.back: Answers vary by district. Look up the member of the U.S. House for your district before your interview. (People in some territories may name a non-voting Delegate or Resident Commissioner.)
cards.quizAnswer: The member of the U.S. House elected from your district.
distractors: One of your two U.S. Senators.
The governor of your home state.
A member of your state legislature.
An elector chosen for your state.

cards.front: Who does a U.S. Senator represent?
cards.back: All people of the state. Unlike a Representative, who serves one district, a Senator represents the entire state.
cards.quizAnswer: All the people of the Senator's state.
distractors: Only the people of one district.
Only the members of the Senator's party.
Only the citizens who voted for them.
The voters in the state capital.

cards.front: Why do some states have more Representatives than other states?
cards.back: Because of the state's population. States with more people get more seats in the House; every state's Senate count stays at two.
cards.quizAnswer: Because of the state's population (more people, more Representatives).
distractors: Because of the state's land area.
Because of the state's age and wealth.
Because larger states pay more taxes.
Because of the number of registered voters.
Because the Constitution lists them that way.

cards.front: We elect a President for how many years?
cards.back: Four (4) years. A President may be elected to no more than two terms under the 22nd Amendment.

cards.front: In what month do we vote for President?
cards.back: November. Presidential elections are held on the Tuesday after the first Monday in November every four years.

cards.front: What is the name of the President of the United States now?
cards.back: As of 2026, Donald J. Trump. This answer changes with each new President — confirm who is serving at the time of your interview.
cards.quizAnswer: Donald J. Trump (as of 2026 — verify before your interview).

cards.front: What is the name of the Vice President of the United States now?
cards.back: As of 2026, J.D. Vance. This answer changes with each administration — confirm who is serving at the time of your interview.
cards.quizAnswer: J.D. Vance (as of 2026 — verify before your interview).

cards.front: If the President can no longer serve, who becomes President?
cards.back: The Vice President. The Vice President is first in the line of presidential succession.

cards.front: If both the President and the Vice President can no longer serve, who becomes President?
cards.back: The Speaker of the House. The Speaker is next in line after the Vice President under the presidential succession law.
distractors: The Chief Justice of the Supreme Court.
The President pro tempore of the Senate.

cards.front: Who is the Commander in Chief of the military?
cards.back: The President. The Constitution makes the civilian President the head of the armed forces.

cards.front: Who signs bills to become laws?
cards.back: The President. After both houses of Congress pass a bill, the President can sign it into law (or veto it).

cards.front: Who vetoes bills?
cards.back: The President. A veto rejects a bill; Congress can override a veto with a two-thirds vote in both houses.

cards.front: What does the President's Cabinet do?
cards.back: Advises the President. The Cabinet is made up of the heads of the executive departments, who give the President advice on their areas.

cards.front: What are two Cabinet-level positions?
cards.back: Examples include Secretary of State, Secretary of Defense, Secretary of the Treasury, Attorney General, Secretary of Education, and others. Any two are accepted.
cards.quizAnswer: Secretary of State and Secretary of Defense (among others).
distractors: Speaker of the House and Senate Majority Leader.
Vice President and Chief of Staff.
